coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots intensified poly (ethylene glycol)/chitosan/sodium glycerophosphate hydrogel as artificial synovium tissue with slow-release lubricant
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Mechanical Behavior of Biomedical Materials
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Hailin
Lv, Leifeng
Ma, Jun
Ban, Wenrui
Ren, Shanshan
Dong, Guangneng
Li, Jianhui
Dang, Xiaoqian
coredata.dc:description: The ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene (UHMWPE) and metal artificial joint pair is limited by wear debris and short service life. Here we report the development of a hydrogel which exhibits lubricant release to intensify the lubrication effect of artificial joints.This study adopted an injectable method to prepare carbon dots/poly (ethylene glycol)/chitosan/sodium glycerophosphate (CDs/PEG/CS/GP) composite hydrogel, and the carbon dots were used to intensify the rheological and mechanical properties. In addition, the composite hydrogel had slow-release properties, and the release solution contained CDs, PEG and GP has excellent lubrication effect. At last, the MTT assay, LIVE/DEAD staining, H&E staining results and safety evaluation in BALC/c mice proved that the hydrogels had good biocompatibilility and were safety for application in vivo.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Lubricant
Slow-release
Fluorescent

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Safety evaluation by hydrohydrogels implanted in BLAC/c mice
The lubrication effect of different materials
The slow-release effect and relevant mechanisms
Cytocompatibility of the hydrohydrogels by MTT assay and LIVE/DEAD staining
Histomorphological evaluation of implantation associated reject action

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image of CDs, (b) The fluorescence spectra of 0.5 wt% CDs, (c) The XPS spectrum of CDs and (d) The high resolution XPS of N 1s.
(a) The FT-IR spectra and (b) The XRD patterns.
The rheological analyses of PEG/CS/GP and CDs/PEG/CS/GP.
(a) The shear stress and (b) The viscosity of PEG/CS/GP and CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel.
(a) The transmittance of PEG/CS/GP release solution and CDs/PEG/CS/GP release solution and (b) The fluorescent spectrum of CDs/PEG/CS/GP release solution.

(a) The kinematic viscosity and (b) The average friction coefficient of different solutions (distilled water, 5 wt% GP, 5 wt% PEG, P﻿E﻿G﻿/﻿G﻿P﻿ ﻿(5 wt%/5 wt%﻿)﻿ ﻿a﻿n﻿d﻿ ﻿C﻿D﻿s﻿/﻿P﻿E﻿G﻿/﻿G﻿P﻿ (0.5 wt%/5 wt%/5 wt%)﻿. (c) The average friction coefficient of different GP concentrations. (d) The average friction coefficient of different PEG concentrations.
(a) Standing released for different day, (b) Released under different rotation speed and (c) The crosslinking of CDs, PEG and GP in release solution.

(a) Cell viability by MTT assay. The results are displayed as mean ± SEM (error bars). Statistical analyses were done by one-way ANOVA, (b)–(f) LIVE/DEAD staining results(×100), green cells were live while yellow cells were dead:(b) PEG/CS/GP(1:3) group, (c) PEG/CS/GP(1:2) group, (d) CDs/PEG/CS/GP(1:3) group, (e) CDs/PEG/CS/GP (1:2) group, (f) control group.
H&E staining results(×200): (a) PEG/CS/GP(1:3) group, (b) PEG/CS/GP(1:2) group, (c) CDs/PEG/CS/GP(1:3) group, (d) CDs/PEG/CS/GP (1:2) group, (e) control group.

(a) Aspartate aminotransferase (AST) level, (b) alanine aminotransferase (ALT) level, (c) creatinine level, (d) blood uric acid (BUA) level. Red dotted lines show the upper and lower limit value of normal reference value. All results are displayed as mean ± SEM (error bars). Statistical analyses were done by one-way ANOVA.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots intensified poly (ethylene glycol)/chitosan/sodium glycerophosphate hydrogel as artificial synovium tissue with slow-release lubricant

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Modern Design & Rotor-Bearing System (Xi'an Jiaotong University), Ministry of Education, School of Mechanical Engineering, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, Shaanxi 710049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Modern Design & Rotor-Bearing System (Xi'an Jiaotong University), Ministry of Education, School of Mechanical Engineering, Xi'an Jiaotong University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, Shaanxi 710049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Science, Xi’an Jiaotong University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthopaedics, The Second Affiliated hospital of Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, Shaanxi 710061, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Orthopaedics, The Second Affiliated hospital of Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an Jiaotong University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene (UHMWPE) and metal artificial joint pair is limited by wear debris and short service life. Here we report the development of a hydrogel which exhibits lubricant release to intensify the lubrication effect of artificial joints.This study adopted an injectable method to prepare carbon dots/poly (ethylene glycol)/chitosan/sodium glycerophosphate (CDs/PEG/CS/GP) composite hydrogel, and the carbon dots were used to intensify the rheological and mechanical properties. In addition, the composite hydrogel had slow-release properties, and the release solution contained CDs, PEG and GP has excellent lubrication effect. At last, the MTT assay, LIVE/DEAD staining, H&E staining results and safety evaluation in BALC/c mice proved that the hydrogels had good biocompatibilility and were safety for application in vivo.

CDs, PEG and GP have hydroxyls and they would crosslink with each other via hydrogen bond effect.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: The arthroplasty brings the well-being to the patients, but the synovium tissue was removed after joint replacement surgery and the body fluid became the only lubrication solution which showed limited lubricating ability (Bruce and Walmsley, 1937). Ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene (UHMWPE) has been used in joint replacements as a bearing material for five decades (Deng and Xiong, 2015). However, the wear debris will be produced by the wear of UHMWPE, and more excessive microscopic wear debris will be generated by prosthetic joint into the joint space due to the poor lubrication effect of body fluid, and the UHMWPE wear debris further caused the inflammatory and osteolysis (Gupta et al., 2007; Jacobs et al., 2001; Muratoglu et al., 2005). In addition, the aseptic loosening phenomenon was triggered by the UHMWPE wear debris in artificial joint replacement (Collins et al., 2013; Guo et al., 2017; Jiang et al., 2013; Kobayashi and Hyu, 2010).

As a result, more wear debris were generated due to the poor lubrication effect of the body fluid, the service life of artificial joints became shorter. Traditional lubricant will be diluted by body fluid and then be adsorbed by human body, and the patients suffered more pain and infection risk from frequent lubricant injection (Watson et al., 2015). As a thermo-sensitive hydrogel, chitosan/sodium glycerophosphate (CS/GP) hydrogel was first reported by Chenite et al. (2000). However, this kind of hydrogel has the properties of poor control hydrogel formation, low compressive strength and loose structure (Li et al., 2014a). But the strength and anti-pressure abilities are important for application on artificial joints. Because the hydrogel may be extruded by muscle in vivo body, the intense exercises and accidents will disperse the hydrogel quickly. The dispersed hydrogel will be corroded by body fluid and degrade to small part, the degraded hydrogel further be diluted by body fluid and then be adsorbed by vivo body. Therefore, high strength hydrogel exists for long-term in vivo body is very important for artificial joints. Long existence time cause the strength hydrogel to have obviously advantages than traditional lubricant for long term application on artificial joints. So, carbon dots/poly (ethylene glycol) (CDs/PEG) solution which had good lubrication effect was added into CS/GP to form an injectable thermo-sensitive hydrogel: carbon dots/poly (ethylene glycol)/chitosan/sodium glycerophosphate (CDs/PEG/CS/GP), and this new hydrogel has better rheological properties than CS/GP hydrogel. Chitosan (CS), sodium glycerophosphate (GP), carbon dots (CDs) and poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) have been widely used in human body because of their properties of biocompatibility, biodegradability, non-toxicity (Baker and Baker, 2010; Chenite et al., 2000; IY et al., 2008; Jiao et al., 2016; Li et al., 2014a; Lu et al., 2017a, 2018, 2017b; Ma et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2004; Zhang and Neau, 2002; Zhang et al., 2012; Zhou et al., 2015).

Injectable hydrogels have been widely-used in the treatment of soft tissue restoration. It can be utilized in soft tissue, such as tissue cavities, wrinkles and skin patches. For effective clinical application, the hydrogel should be formed in mild physiological conditions and the spending time should be in a clinically relevant time period, and the hydrogel should have non-toxicity, biodegradability and biocompatibility (Gao et al., 2010). From a clinical perspective, the injectable CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel is more attractive than a preformed hydrogel. Because the injectable method can minimize patient discomfort, treatment cost, scar formation and infection risk (Hou et al., 2004). Furthermore the in-situ formation hydrogel is thermo-pH responsive, and it achieves acceptable degree of harm to other tissues of the human body (Williams, 2008). Joshi et al. (2018) represented an attractive paradigm in inflammatory arthritis therapy, hydrogel loaded with triamcinolone acetonide releases drug on demand upon exposure to enzymes or synovial fluid from patients with rheumatoid arthritis.

In this study, we designed an injectable method for the fabrication of CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel. Notably, the CDs and PEG were slow-released from the CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel and the tribology tests were used to analysis the lubrication effect. The cytocompatibility of the CDs/PEG/CS/GP composite hydrogel was investigated by MTT assay and LIVE/DEAD staining, and then the histomorphological evaluation of implantation associated reject action and safety evaluation in BALC/c mice were used to prove its safety. This technology prepared an excellent slow-release hydrogel via an injectable method, and this CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel could enhance the lubrication effect and prolong the service life of artificial joins efficiently.

Poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG, molecular weight, 4000) was purchased from Guangdong Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Chitosan (CS, degree of deacetylation ≥ 90%; 700–800 kDa) was purchased from Shanghai Lanji technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the materials in the research were analytic reagent grade (AR).

The preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots: 1.2 g DL-alanine was added into 30 mL deionized water and stirred for 30 min at room temperature to yield a clear solution, and then the mixture was added into the reaction vessel of the microwave reactor. Elevate the temperature of the reaction vessel up to 200 °C in 5 min. The reaction vessel was maintained at 200 °C for 1 h with microwave irradiation and then cooled to room temperature. The transparent aqueous solution was subjected to dialysis (Mw = 1000) for 7 days to eliminate the overreacted residue.

To prepare CDs/PEG/CS/GP gel, 0.005 g CDs, 0.3 g PEG and 0.02 g CS were added in a bottle, and then 0.675 mL 1 wt% acetic acid solution was added, the mixture was stirred at 500 rpm for 1 h to obtain a homogeneous CDs/PEG/CS (0.5 wt%/2 wt%/30 wt%) solution. Next, 0.7 g GP was added in 0.3 mL distilled water and heated to obtain a 70 wt% solution, which was then kept at the temperature of 37 °C. Then 0.25 g GP solution and 1 g CDs/CS/PEG sol were mixed at 37 °C for 1 h to obtain CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel. The PEG/CS/GP hydrogel was obtained on the same condition.

The tribological properties were investigated using a ball-on-disk tribometer (UMT-2, CETR Corporation Ltd, USA), and the samples were slide against the disk with the reciprocation friction drive system at room temperature (25 °C). The disk was CoCrMo alloy (Φ 30 mm, surface roughness 0.017 µm), and the UHMWPE ball (Φ9.5 mm, surface roughness 0.12 µm) was used to slide against the disk. The load was 5 N (10.9 MPa), and the time was 15 min, and the sliding speed was 24 mm s−1. All the tests were repeated five times and the results were averaged.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) was carried out between 400 and 4000 cm−1 using a Bruker spectrophotometer (TENSOR27, Bruker, Germany). FT-IR is an effective method to investigate specific interaction. X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization was used to investigate the crystal phase structure patterns (D8 advance, Bruker, Germany). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to observe the dried hydrogels (Gemini SEM 500, Zeiss, Germany). The ultraviolet/visible (UV/vis) spectra were used to measure the transmittance of slow-release solution (Hitachi U-4100). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEM-2100Plus, JEOL, Japan) was used to observe the morphology. Fluorescence analysis (QuantaMaster400, PTI, USA) was used to observe emission wavelength shifted from 350 nm to 600 nm when the excitation wavelength escalated from 360 nm to 450 nm.

The cytotoxicity of hydrohydrogels were measured by employing a direct contact test between hydrohydrogels and L929 cells. To better investigate the cytotoxicity and safety of different hydrogels, we set four group treated with hydrogels containing different material proportion. As described before in the section of hydrohydrogel preparation, GP solution and other solution sterilized by filtration (0.22 µm filter, Millipore). The mixture was poured into a sterile petri dish to form a hydrohydrogel film with about 1.5 mm thickness at 37 °C in CO2 incubator. Then hydrohydrogel film was cut into 3.5 mm diameter slices using a Harris Micro-Punch (Whatman, USA). The RPMI1640 (Gibco) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (Gibco), 1.0 × 105 U/L penicillin (Hyclone) and 100 mg/L streptomycin (Hyclone) was used as the complete medium. L929 cells were seeded in 96-well plate at a density of 12,000 cells/well. After cultured for 24 h, the hydrohydrogel slices were introduced into the wells. The cell proliferation and viability under the hydrohydrogel was evaluated by MTT assay and LIVE/DEAD® Viability/Cytotoxicity Kit assay after 24 h. MTT assay was executed as the protocol by Wilson (Masters, 2000). OD value was detected at 540 nm using a microplate reader (FLUOstar Omega) according to the manufacturer's instructions. Tests were repeated three times for each group. The cell viability was evaluated by Live/Dead staining, HUVECs were plated into 96-well plates at a density of 5000 cells/well. Following 24 h of intervention, the cells were treated with staining solution. The staining solution was prepared according to LIVE/DEAD® Viability/Cytotoxicity Kit (Molecular Probes) specification. Calcein AM and ethidium homodimer-1(Molecular Probes) was thawed and diluted with DMEM. The cells were treated with staining solution for 45 min at 37 °C and observed by inverted fluorescence microscope (Leica, USA). Live cells were green under blue excitation light and dead cells were red under green excitation light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Safety evaluation by hydrohydrogels implanted in BLAC/c mice
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the safety in vivo and acute rejection reaction in situ, hydrohydrogels were implanted in muscle bag of gluteus maximus and prepared by the method above. Our animal experiments were approved by the institutional review board of Xi’an Jiaotong University. Male BLAC/c mice weighting 25–30 g and 5–6-week age were used for studies. 3.5 mm diameter hydrohydrogel slices for implantation were prepared as the method above.

All mice were randomly divided into groups including control and hydrohydrogels group. Every group contains 8 mice. All mice were acclimatized for 1 week before surgery. All surgery procedures were performed under aseptic condition. After standard anesthesia procedure with intraperitoneal injection of 7 wt% chloral hydrate (3 mL/kg body weight), After shaved and sterilized, 5 mm diameter skin incision were created and fasciae were cut. Hydrohydrogels for implantation were injected in the muscle bag and all wounds were closed gently. Mice in control group were given sham operation. We labeled the location of materials by photos.

Muscle tissue and Major organs were quickly harvested from the body at 1 week after surgery and immersed in 4 wt% paraformaldehyde for 48 h. The fixed organs were then paraffin-embedded and 5 mm thick histological sections were prepared (obtained from the UNC Histopathology Core Facility). The paraffin sections were mounted onto slides and stained with hematoxylin and eosin. The morphological features of tissues were observed under a light microscope (Leica, USA) and captured by image analysis system for the analysis of tissue injuries.

For the biochemical analysis, serum samples were collected and assessed for hepatic and renal function, included aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), blood uric acid (BUA), and creatinine was evaluated by using a commercial kit (Jiancheng Bioengineering, China). All operations were strictly followed manufacture's instruction.

The CDs were evaluated by TEM as Fig. 1a shown. The as-prepared CDs nanoparticles were aggregation, these particles have different sizes and most of them below 10 nm. Fig. 1b shows the fluorescence spectra of CDs, and the emission wavelength shifted from 350 nm to 600 nm when the excitation wavelength escalated from 360 nm to 450 nm. The strongest emission intensity was at 455 nm when used 390 nm excitation wavelength, this phenomena could be due to the emissions from different size CDs (Russo et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2015). XPS characterization was used to analyze the composition of elements and their chemical states. The atoms of C, N, and O in CDs were detected at the peaks of ca. 283 eV, 399 eV, and 531 eV, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1c. Fig. 1d shows the high resolution XPS of N 1s, and the N 1s spectrum could be divided into two main peaks: 398.5 eV of pyridinic N and 399.3 eV of lactam and imide N (Chen et al., 2011), which proved that the CDs was nitrogen-doped.

Fig. 2a shows the FT-IR spectra of CDs, PEG, CS, GP and CDs/PEG/CS/GP. In Fig. 2a, the characteristic adsorption band at 1070 cm−1 was attributed to C-O-C functional groups and the absorption bands at 2871 cm−1 was belong to C-H stretch of CH2 (Hussein et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2009), PEG and CS exhibited the same absorption peaks at 2860 and 1050 cm−1 because of their same functional groups. PEG has the characteristic peak at 955 and 842 cm−1 can be attributed to the crystal band and the C-C-O bonds (Abu-Zied et al., 2015; Hussein et al., 2013). In the spectrum of GP, the characteristic peak around 1049 cm−1 represented the -PO4 2- group, and the stretching peak about 1668 cm−1 was due to vibration of C˭O (Li et al., 2014b). The band at 1591 cm−1 was due to the C˭C in CDs (Zou et al., 2016). The spectrum of GP was similar to the CDs/PEG/CS/GP, which indicated that most of CDs, PEG and CS participated in the intermolecular interaction, which caused the disappearance of these characteristic peaks (Lu et al., 2017a, 2017b).

0 wt% CDs, 0.24 wt% CDs, 0.4 wt% and 0.8 wt% CDs of CDs/CS/PEG/GP hydrogels were added in drying oven to remove moisture at 37 °C, and then XRD characterizations were performed to investigate the crystal phase structure as Fig. 2b shown. The crystalline PEG powder showed intensive X-ray patterns at about 19.2° and 23.3°, in coincidence with previous studies (Ozeki et al., 1997; Schachter et al., 2004; Wulff et al., 1996). And the XRD pattern of CDs gives a sharp peak at 20.8° which indicated densely packed alkyl groups (Baker and Baker, 2010; Bourlinos et al., 2008), the diffraction peaks at about 2θ = 20.1° are attribute to the diffraction of CS matrix (Hyder and Chen, 2009; Lu et al., 2015). The CDs and CS had low proportion in CDs/CS/PEG/GP material, which caused the peaks so weak that they couldn’t be found in Fig. 2b. But PEG had high proportion in the CDs/PEG/CS/GP material, and the 0 wt% composite (CS/PEG/GP) had the strongest PEG peaks, the materials had two peaks at 19.2° and 23.3° which were located at the same position with the PEG. Because the CDs, PEG, CS and GP underwent intermolecular interactions during the hydrogelation process via hydrogen bond effect, and the final composition of these four materials did not participate in the reaction completely, especially the PEG. But the PEG peaks had lower intensity with the CDs proportion increasing. It revealed that CDs can effectively crosslink with the PEG and decreased the PEG's crystallinity, and further improved the participation ratio of PEG in the reaction process.

Fig. 3 shows the rheological analyses of PEG/CS/GP and CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogels at 37 °C. Because the elastic behaviour of the hydrogels was solid state, it caused the storage modulus were higher than the loss modulus (Fig. 3a). In Fig. 3a, the CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel had higher storage modulus than PEG/CS/GP hydrogel. Meanwhile the CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel had higher loss modulus in Fig. 3c, and the storage modulus and loss modulus obviously increased with the angular frequency adding. And the complex viscosity was obviously improved after adding CDs in Fig. 3d, indicating that the CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel had better shear resistance property than PEG/CS/GP hydrogel. And the addition of CDs caused the CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel had better intermolecular interactions during the hydrogelation process.

The artificial joints may endure high shear pressure and high shear rate due to intense exercises and accidents, thus it is important to study the slow-release effect under pressure. In Fig. 4, the CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel has higher shear stress (Fig. 4a) and higher viscosity (Fig. 4b) than the PEG/CS/GP hydrogel. The shear stress and viscosity decreased with the increasing of shear rate because of shear thinning. The CDs, PEG, CS and GP formed a network which fell apart and individual hydrogel fragments start to flow when exposed to increasing shear rate. I﻿n﻿ C﻿D﻿s﻿/﻿P﻿E﻿G﻿/﻿C﻿S﻿/﻿G﻿P hydrogel, the CDs which have hydroxyls associated with PEG, CS and GP formed an entangled network, and then the shear stress and viscosity became higher than PEG/CS/GP hydrogel. As a result, CDs could improve the rheological properties of CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel effectively.

The PEG/CS/GP release solution and CDs/PEG/CS/GP release solution were characterized by the UV/vis transmittance spectra. Fig. 5a showed that the release solution from the PEG/CS/GP hydrogel had higher transmittance than from the CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel. Maybe, the CDs caused the CDs/PEG/CS/GP release solution had lower transmittance than PEGCS/GP release solution. So, fluorescent spectrum was used to detect the CDs from the CDs/PEG/CS/GP release solution, and Fig. 5b confirmed that the CDs/PEG/CS/GP release solution contained CDs. The colored CDs release solution could be excited by ultravioler light and showed blue lubminescence, and it caused the transmittance was very low between 200 and 380 nm wavelength as Fig. 5a shown.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The lubrication effect of different materials
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6a shows the kinematic viscosities of different materials. The kinematic viscosities of distilled water, 5 wt% GP, 5 wt% PEG, PEG/GP (5 wt%/5 wt%) and CDs/PEG/GP (0.5 wt%/5 wt%/5 wt%) were 0.99, 1.02, 1.49, 1.85 and 3.58 mm2 s−1, respectively. In Fig. 6b, the above materials had the average friction coefficient of 0.061, 0.035, 0.025, 0.019 and 0.012, respectively. The CDs/PEG/GP sol had highest kinematic viscosity and lowest average friction coefficient in these materials. In Fig. 6c and d, the average friction coefficient of GP and PEG solution decreased with the concentration increasing, but the CDs/PEG/GP sol had obviously better lubrication effect than them. The results showed that the better lubricant was obtained when CDs, PEG and GP were mixed than when either used alone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The slow-release effect and relevant mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para: The service time of artificial joints will be affected by the slow-release properties of CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel. In this study, 1.25 g CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel was added into 2 mL distilled water. Ten prepared samples were standing released for 1–10 days, respectively. Then the lubrication performances of the releasing solution were investigated, and the release solutions from the PEG/CS/GP hydrogel were also investigated under the same conditions. Fig. 7a shows that the slow-release solution from CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel had better lubrication effect than from PEG/CS/GP hydrogel. As discussed in Section 3.5, PEG and GP had good lubrication effect with low concentration, and CDs, PEG mixed with GP had better lubrication effect than either used alone. So, the CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel release solution contained CDs had better lubrication effect. The lubrication effect of release solution did not stabilize over the course of 10 days, which indicated that the release process occurred slowly.

Because the CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel may be extruded by muscle in vivo body, standing release is not sufficient for application on artificial joints. Therefore, we used different rotate speeds to simulate the extrusion pressure with a centrifugal machine. In this study, 1.25 g CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel was added in 2.0 mL distilled water. Eight prepared samples were centrifuged with rotate speeds from 500 to 4000 rpm for 10 min. Then the tribology properties of the release solutions were tested. The release solutions from PEG/CS/GP hydrogel were tested under the same conditions. In Fig. 7b, the pressure increased with the increasing rotation speed, and the pressure caused the hydrogels to release more lubricant in a short time. However, the rotation release solution had obviously better lubrication effect than the standing release solution in a short time. In addition, the CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel release solution had better lubrication effect than PEG/CS/GP hydrogel release solution, because the CDs which were released from CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel had hydroxyls, and they would crosslink with GP and PEG via hydrogen bond. In Fig. 7a and b, the average friction coefficient proved that the hydrogels released slowly on normal condition, but it would release faster under pressure.

CDs, PEG and GP contain hydroxyls, so they will crosslink with each other via hydrogen bonds to form a bonded network structure as Fig. 7c shown. The CDs/PEG/GP chains became longer and curlier than pure PEG chains, the entangled networks were formed in CDs/PEG/CS/GP release solution. The CDs, PEG and GP mixed to obtain better lubrication effect than either used alone because of the broader networks were formed, thus the bonded networks caused the release solution had higher viscosity and lower average friction coefficient. The above data revealed that the CDs, PEG and GP could crosslink with each other via hydrogen bonds may be the primary factors which caused the release solution to have a good lubrication effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytocompatibility of the hydrohydrogels by MTT assay and LIVE/DEAD staining
ce:section.ce:para: The cytocompatibility of the hydrohydrogels was further evaluated by testing the cytotoxicity of the hydrohydrogels. To better investigate the cytotoxicity of different hydrogels, we set four group treated with hydrogels containing different material proportion. All of the four hydrohydrogels (PEG/CS/GP(1:3), PEG/CS/GP(1:2),CDs/PEG/CS/GP(1:3),CDs/PEG/CS/GP(1:2))showed more than 100% of cell viability compared to that of control group ( p > 0.05) ( Fig. 8a). The LIVE/DEAD staining results (Fig. 8b–f) were in consistence with the cell viability results. The cells in hydrohydrogel groups were green and showed a spindle-like morphology similar to the control group. Few dead cells appeared in all of groups. Both the cell viability and LIVE/DEAD staining results demonstrated that the hydrohydrogels had good cytocompatibility. Based on the results above, these hydrohydrogels possessed cytocompatibility for joint lubrication application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Histomorphological evaluation of implantation associated reject action
ce:section.ce:para: Acute implantation associated reject action is one of the most frequently and life threatened complication during biomaterials implantation. Histomorphological manifestations of acute rejection reaction in situ shows connective tissue edema, infiltration of inflammatory cells and necrocytosis. In all four hydrohydrogels groups, muscle tissues around implantation materials showed normal morphology without necrosis and cell degeneration ( Fig. 9). Little macrophages or monocytes could be found among muscle cells. However, we hardly got the paraffin sections containing with hydrohydrogels materials due to the size of fixed samples. We preferred to investigate histology changes of soft tissues around implantation area rather than materials. H&E staining results showed hydrohydrogels didn’t induce acute implantation associated reject action in mice.

In addition, we further investigated the safety of hydrohydrogels. No decrease in body weight was observed in any of the groups, indicating that there was no apparent toxicity associated with the treatments. The H&E staining results of the toxicological assay showed that major organs including heart, liver, spleen, lungs and kidneys did not display any significant changes in morphology for control or hydrohydrogels groups. The hepatic and renal function data indicated that the liver functional parameters aspartate aminotransferase (AST) level, alanine aminotransferase (ALT), blood uric acid (BUA), and creatinine of mice in four hydrohydrogels group were all located in the normal reference ranges ( Fig. 10) (Nigam, 2006). Materials implantation did not influence liver and renal function in the mouse model, which furtherly support that hydrohydrogels are safety for application in vivo.

In this study, fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots were prepared through a one-step microwave process using DL-alanine as the precursor, and the nitrogen-doped carbon dots showed strong blue lubminescence when excited with ultravioler light, and the CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel was prepared. The tribological results showed that the release process was very slow, and the CDs/PEG/CS/GP release solution had better lubrication ability than the PEG/CS/GP release solution. The prepared hydrogel was released slowly under normal conditions, but it was released faster under pressure. CDs, PEG and GP crosslink with each other via hydrogen bonds, which may be the primary factors that caused the release solution to have good lubrication ability. Meanwhile, CDs improved the rheological and mechanical properties of CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel because of CDs caused the composite hydrogel to have better intermolecular interactions. Futhermore, the cytocompatibility tests, histomorphological evaluation and safety evaluation proved that the composite hydrogel was safety for application in vivo. The injectable CDs/PEG/CS/GP hydrogel was easy to prepare, which exhibited great advantages as an artificial synovium tissue to prolong the service time of artificial joints.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51475358, 81572147).

ce:section.ce:para: All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript. H.L.L. and L.F.L. contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.
